text.title: Three Core VUI Design Principles with Examples
text.summary: The three foundational VUI principles — simplicity, natural conversation, and context awareness — with good/bad dialogue examples.

rule.statement: Every agent turn must convey one piece of information or request one piece of input; split compound responses across turns.
rule.rationale: Listening comprehension is harder than reading; users cannot re-read a voice response, so each turn must be immediately parseable in working memory.

example.code: System: "Your order is confirmed for delivery tomorrow."
System: "Your order has been successfully processed and scheduled for delivery on January 20th at a time between 9 AM and 5 PM in your local timezone. You will receive a confirmation email shortly."
example.note: Too much information in a single turn; the user cannot retain all of it and cannot re-listen.

rule.statement: Design agent turns to follow natural conversational turn-taking: acknowledge the user's input briefly, then provide the response or request, without robotic enumerations.
rule.rationale: Users calibrate their interaction style to the agent's tone; robotic responses trigger a robotic, stilted interaction from the user, increasing ASR errors.

example.code: User: "What's the weather?" VUI: "It's 72 and sunny. Want to know about tomorrow?"
User: "What's the weather?" VUI: "The current temperature is 72 degrees Fahrenheit with sunny conditions. Humidity is 45 percent. Wind speed is 8 miles per hour."
example.note: Reciting all available data fields is a database-readout pattern, not a conversation.

antipattern.description: LLM over-acknowledgment: "Sure! I can absolutely do that for you! Let me check that right away!"
antipattern.reason: Filler acknowledgment inflates latency — every word adds ~50ms TTS overhead — and feels sycophantic. One-word acknowledgment ("Got it.") or no acknowledgment before the answer is preferred.

rule.statement: The agent must correctly resolve pronoun references from the immediately preceding user turn without asking for clarification more than once per session.
rule.rationale: Users expect the agent to understand "it", "that", "the one I mentioned" — repeated clarification requests signal that the agent forgot context and are perceived as system failure.

list.item: Remember previous conversation turns within the session to resolve follow-up questions.
Understand ellipsis and pronoun references ("And tomorrow?" after a weather query).
Adapt to stated user preferences within the session (e.g., user said "I prefer Celsius" → use Celsius for all subsequent queries).

antipattern.description: Context window exhaustion causes the agent to ask the user to repeat information already provided earlier in the session.
antipattern.reason: Context limits (~30k tokens for Realtime API) are a design constraint; the session structure must proactively summarize and compress prior context before limits are hit.
